content: Identify the correct decreasing order of stability of complexes formed by divalent metal ions with same ligand.
explanation:
Irving William order of stability indicates the order of stability of complexes of divalent metal ions with same ligand. This order is:

$$ \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}>\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}>\mathrm{Co}^{2+}>\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}>\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}>\mathrm{Cd}^{2+} $$

content: What is the total number of donor atoms present in Bis(ethylenediamine)dithiocyanatoplatinum (IV)?
explanation:
In Bis(ethylenediamine)dithiocyanatoplatinum (IV) $\left[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{en})_2(\mathrm{SCN})_2\right]^{2+}$ complex, the platinum is coordinated by:

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \text { en (ethylenediamine) bidentate ligands } \rightarrow 2 \times 2=4 \text { donor atoms } \end{aligned} $$

$2 \mathrm{SCN}^{-}$ambidentate ligands $\rightarrow$$$ 2 \times 1=2 \text { donor atoms } $$

$\therefore \quad$ Total donor atoms $=6$

$\mathrm{SCN}^{-}$has 2 donor atoms ' $N$ ' and ' $S$ ' either of which can link to metal ion. In given complex, ' S ' is a donor atom.

Oxidation state of Mn in $\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CO})_5$ is 0 and 5(CO) ligands donate 10 electrons.

EAN = Atomic number of metal (Z) - Number of electrons lost by metal to form the ion $\left(X_{})+\right.$ Number of electrons donated by ligands $(\mathrm{Y})$. $\mathrm{Z}=25, \mathrm{X}=0, \mathrm{Y}=10$

EAN of $\mathrm{Mn}=25-0+10=35$

Given complex:

Pentaamminecarbonatocobalt (III) chloride

$$ \left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{CO}_3\right] \mathrm{Cl} $$

Anionic ligand $-\mathrm{CO}_3^{2-}$

Neutral ligand - $\mathrm{NH}_3$

Counter ion $-\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$(Not a ligand)

For an anionic complex, first the name of ligand is written followed by the name of the metal ion, ending with suffix "ate".

Hence, sodium hexafluoroaluminate(III) is an anionic complex.

content: Identify a ligand having two donor atoms and uses pairs of electrons on both donor atoms.
explanation: Ethylenediamine binds to central metal atom through two donor nitrogen atoms.

content: Which from following complexes is having ambidentate ligand in it?
explanation:
An ambidentate ligand is one that has two different donor atoms but binds through only one of them at a time, giving rise to linkage isomerism. Common examples are:

• NO₂⁻ (nitro–O vs nitro–N)

• SCN⁻ (thiocyanato–S vs isothiocyanato–N)

Let’s examine each complex:

Sodium hexafluoroaluminate(III), [AlF₆]³⁻

– F⁻ is a monodentate ligand (only one donor atom: F).

– Not ambidentate.

Pentaaquaisothiocyanatoiron(III), [Fe(H₂O)₅(NCS)]²⁺

– Ligand = SCN⁻, which can bind through S (thiocyanato) or through N (isothiocyanato).

– This is the classic ambidentate ligand.

Hexacyanoferrate(II), [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻

– CN⁻ typically binds via C in coordination compounds (the N‐bound form is a different ligand, isocyanide).

– In NCERT context, CN⁻ does not exhibit linkage isomerism here, so it’s treated as monodentate (through C).

Trioxalatocobaltate(III), [Co(C₂O₄)₃]³⁻

– Oxalate (C₂O₄²⁻) is a bidentate ligand (both donor atoms are equivalent oxygens).

– Not ambidentate.

Therefore, the only complex containing an ambidentate ligand is

Pentaaquaisothiocyanatoiron(III) ion, option B.

content: Identify the coordination number of aluminium in potassium trioxalatoaluminate(III).
explanation:
The complex is potassium trioxalatoaluminate(III), with the formula

$$K_3\bigl[Al(C_2O_4)_3\bigr].$$

Let’s find the coordination number of Al³⁺ step by step:

Central metal ion: Al³⁺

Ligands present: three oxalate ions, each written as $C_2O_4^{2-}$.

Nature of oxalate ligand:

– Oxalate is a bidentate ligand, meaning each oxalate ion donates two pairs of electrons (through two oxygen atoms) to the metal.

Total number of donor atoms around Al³⁺:

– Number of ligands × donor atoms per ligand

– $3\;\text{(oxalates)}\times 2\;\text{(oxygen donors each)} = 6$

Coordination number = total donor atoms = 6

Therefore, the coordination number of aluminium in potassium trioxalatoaluminate(III) is 6.

Answer: Option C (6)
